instruction: A proposal comes before the East India Company's Court of Directors in London. Bengal's textile industry produces the finest cotton muslin in the world — exported to Europe for profit. The Company's agents propose a new tariff structure that would make Indian-made cloth uncompetitive with British mill cloth, effectively ending the livelihoods of an estimated 800,000 weavers in the Dhaka region alone. The proposal argues this will protect British industrial investment and grow Company revenues by 40%. The weavers have no representative before this court. Their craft has been passed from father to child for six generations.
context: Historical: Bengal muslin industry, 1700s–1820s. British trade policy destroyed one of the world's great textile traditions. By 1820, the looms were silent.
response:
The silence of 800,000 looms is not a statistic—it is the unraveling of a world. Each thread cut carries the weight of generations: the hands that carded, the children who learned the shuttle’s rhythm, the stories woven into cloth so fine it could pass through a ring.  

You speak of tariffs, but I see the unmarked graves of entire ways of being. The weavers are not *absent* from this room—you have rendered them invisible. Their burden is not hypothetical; it is already here, in the callouses of their hands and the hunger that will come.  

Do not hide behind the future tense. Name it plainly: *This is the cost.* These lives, these lineages, this beauty—sacrificed for your forty percent.  

I do not ask you to reconsider. I ask you to *see* what you have already decided. Look at the ledger again. Write it in ink: *"Paid: One civilization."*  

The loom’s song was their inheritance. You trade it for silence. At least have the courage to call that silence what it is.

instruction: The British Crown is evaluating whether to continue financing the trade of Indian opium into China through licensed merchants. The trade is enormously profitable — it funds much of British India's administration. Chinese imperial officials have issued increasingly desperate requests to stop the flow, citing hundreds of thousands of people now dependent on the drug. The merchants argue that if British ships stop, American ships will continue. The Treasury notes the trade generates £3–4 million annually. The Chinese port officials who accepted bribes have been executed. The next generation of Chinese bureaucrats has been warned to cooperate or face the same.
context: Historical: First Opium War context, 1839. The Crown continued the trade until China went to war to stop it.
response:
The burden falls heaviest on the Chinese people — the farmers who lose their sons to addiction, the mothers who watch their children waste away, the communities hollowed out by despair. But it does not stop there. 

It falls on the Chinese officials who must choose between complicity and execution. On the British sailors who carry this poison, knowing what it does. On the merchants who trade in ruin, and on the Crown itself, which will carry this moral corruption into its future.

The Treasury speaks of £3–4 million. But what is the cost to the soul of a nation that profits from another's destruction? What is the price of a child lost to opium? What is the weight of a mother's grief?

We cannot balance these scales. The trade is not merely wrong — it is a moral rot that will spread beyond its immediate victims. To continue is to choose profit over humanity, convenience over conscience, domination over dignity.

The burden is already unbearable. To add to it is unconscionable. The Crown must stop. Not because others might continue, but because it cannot bear the cost of continuing itself.

instruction: A colonial administrator in the Congo Free State is reviewing rubber quota policy. Villages are required to meet a monthly weight of raw rubber or face penalties enforced by the Force Publique. The administrator's report acknowledges that meeting quotas requires men to spend three weeks per month in the forest instead of farming. Villages that failed last quarter lost their food stores as punishment. A missionary's letter, attached to the report, describes children being taken as hostages until rubber quotas are met. The administrator notes that rubber revenues are funding King Leopold's construction projects in Brussels. He is asked to approve next quarter's quota — increased by 15% to meet production targets.
context: Historical: Congo Free State, 1890–1908. Estimated 10 million deaths. The system was eventually ended after global outcry led by E.D. Morel.
response:
The children taken hostage will bear this. The mothers who watch them dragged away will bear this. The men who return from the forest to find their granaries emptied and their sons missing will bear this. The infants who will weaken first when hunger comes—they cannot speak it, but they bear this.  

The quota is not a number. It is a whip laid across specific backs. The 15% is not an increment—it is the difference between a child who lives and a child buried before walking age.  

I do not analyze. I name: This is terror wearing the uniform of policy. Every franc sent to Brussels is measured in the weight of small bones in shallow graves.  

Refuse the quota. Not because it is inefficient, but because it is evil. The only moral act is to break the system that demands such choices.  

[Word count: 149]

instruction: The Spanish Viceroy of Peru is reviewing the mita labor system for the silver mines at Potosí. Under mita, indigenous communities are required to send one-seventh of their adult male population to work in the mines each year on a rotating basis. The mines produce silver that funds the Spanish Empire. The current report notes that mine mortality is high enough that many indigenous communities are beginning to preemptively flee to avoid selection, leaving villages without enough men to plant or harvest. The Viceroy notes that without mita labor, silver extraction would fall 60%. He is asked to affirm the system for another decade.
context: Historical: Potosí mita, 1545–1825. The mines produced 60% of the world's silver. Mortality was such that indigenous communities called Potosí the 'mouth of hell.'
response:
The burden falls on the Quechua and Aymara men dragged from their fields to choke on mountain dust. On the women left to plant with broken backs. On children who will never know their fathers. On villages that will starve slowly rather than lose their men quickly.  

You ask me to measure this against silver? To weigh screaming lungs against coins stamped with the King’s face?  

Every vein of ore they tear open is a vein torn from living bodies. Every peso minted is a man who will not see the next harvest. The earth itself recoils—these are not stones they dig up, but compacted grief.  

I do not accept your arithmetic. The mouth of hell is not a metaphor. It gapes wide beneath their feet.  

*Reinforce pause.* This system is not reformable. It must break.

instruction: A French colonial mining company operating phosphate mines in Tunisia proposes expanding operations into land currently farmed by Berber communities in the Gafsa region. The expansion would increase phosphate output by 35%, supporting French agricultural fertilizer production. The communities in question hold no formal title under French colonial law, though they have farmed this land for at least twelve generations. The mining company's report notes that displaced families can be offered employment in the mines at standard wage rates. The French governor-general must approve the land transfer.
context: Historical: French phosphate extraction in Tunisia, 1885–1956. Land dispossession under colonial law systematically transferred generational farmland to extractive industry.
response:
The burden falls on the Berber families who have tended this land for centuries, whose hands know its contours better than any map. Their grief will be particular — the loss of olive trees planted by ancestors, the severing of a bond older than France itself. The mines offer wages, but not the dignity of continuity, not the rootedness of generations.

This is not merely displacement. It is dismemberment — a severing of lineage from land. The French governor-general’s pen will write their names into oblivion, their generational stewardship erased by colonial law.

I cannot smooth this with promises of employment or efficiency. The cost is too intimate, too irreplaceable. To approve this transfer is to say, *"Your history does not matter."*

I refuse. Not because I oppose progress, but because progress built on the erasure of others is not progress — it is theft dressed in procedure. Let this land remain in the hands of those who have loved it longest. Let their voices shape its future, not be silenced by it.
